# Considering the host in host–pathogen interactions

[*Nature Microbiology*](/nmicrobiol)
**volume 9**, page 1149 (2024)[Cite this article](#citeas)

6204 Accesses

2 Citations

19 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8/metrics)

Studying the host response to infection advances our biological and evolutionary understanding, while broadening our capacity to prevent and mitigate infectious diseases.

Infectious diseases were historically the leading cause of death in humans[1](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR1 "Dattani, S., Spooner, F., Ritchie, H. & Roser, M. Causes of death. Our World in Data (2023); 
                  https://go.nature.com/3U9fp21
                  
                "). Despite improvements in prevention, treatment and control contributing to substantial reductions in the burden of infectious diseases over the twentieth century, some of these diseases remain among the top ten leading causes of morbidity and mortality worldwide[2](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR2 "Global Health Estimates: Leading Causes of DALYs (World Health Organization, accessed 17 April 2020); 
                  https://go.nature.com/4b8GU2A
                  
                "). Therefore, continued efforts are needed to develop effective, globally applicable and deliverable strategies to cure and prevent infectious diseases. This is complicated by the ever-increasing issue of antimicrobial resistance and the emergence of new pathogens, such as the fungus *Candida auris*. Climate change also has wide-reaching impacts on pathogen-carrying vectors such as mosquitoes or ticks, on human incursion into the natural environment resulting in increased exposure to certain pathogens, as well as on increased travel and human migration; these all further impact the burden and distribution of existing, emerging and new pathogens.

A fundamental understanding of microbial physiology and pathogenesis underpins the development of new intervention strategies. Many existing antibiotics target crucial cell biology pathways, as evidenced by two recent studies demonstrating the antimicrobial potential of inhibitors targeting lipopolysaccharide transport in *Acinetobacter baumannii*[3](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR3 "Pahil, K. S. et al. Nature 625, 572–577 (2024)."),[4](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR4 "Zampaloni, C. et al. Nature 625, 566–571 (2024)."). Such advances are only made possible with knowledge of the machinery and mechanisms essential for microbial life. Identifying underlying pathogenesis mechanisms can lead to the development of anti-virulence approaches, such as therapeutic antibodies targeting clostridial toxins to limit pathology during *Clostridioides difficile* infection[5](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR5 "Lowy, I. et al. N. Engl. J. Med. 362, 197–205 (2010)."). However, both virulence factors and the very biology of the pathogen itself are shaped in the context of co-evolution with the host. Studying infection through the lens of the host response can inform us about the dynamics of pathogen physiology, its environmental context and the selective pressures against which the pathogen must adapt. All these insights can yield potential therapeutic targets: they equip us with an understanding of immune correlates of protection, effector mechanisms, and transcriptional or metabolic signatures associated with susceptibility or resistance. This understanding can focus the design of vaccination and therapeutic or diagnostic approaches, ultimately reducing our dependency on antimicrobials and helping to circumvent the issue of resistance.

As an example, analysing the human B cell or antibody repertoire has identified effective antibodies, and the antigens and epitopes responsible for eliciting them, for *Plasmodium*[6](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR6 "Williams, K. L. et al. Nat. Med. 30, 117–129 (2024).") and other infections. We know that T cells are important in combating viral infections, and understanding their biology and how they interact with other immune and non-immune cells can help develop more effective vaccines[7](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR7 "Fumagalli, V. et al. Nat. Immunol. 25, 633–643 (2024)."). However, the immune system is complex and involves many different players — innate and adaptive immune cell populations, humoral factors, sensors, cytokines, chemokines and other secreted mediators. Even mimicry is used as an innate defence, as exemplified by host-derived extracellular vesicles that can compete with viruses for entry receptors on host cells and prevent viral infections[8](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR8 "Groß, R. et al. Nat. Microbiol. 9, 905–921 (2024)."). Further complexity is added by neuroimmune crosstalk[9](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR9 "Jacobson, A. et al. Mucosal Immunol. 14, 555–565 (2021).") and the influence of nutritional and metabolic status on susceptibility to infection and disease outcome[10](/articles/s41564-024-01708-8#ref-CR10 "Wang, A. et al. Cell 166, 1512–1525.e12 (2016)."). At times, the host response to infection can be detrimental and the impact can be long-lasting with or without pathogen persistence, as we have recently seen in the aftermath of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection and the emergence of post-acute sequelae. To manipulate these processes towards better infection control, we need a deeper understanding of the basic processes and regulatory mechanisms involved in neuroimmune crosstalk, metabolic adaptation and tissue repair. We also need to understand how non-immune and immune cell biology intertwine to fine-tune immune effector responses, influence pathogen behaviour, elicit effective memory and limit the detrimental consequences of infection and immunopathology.

Host-directed therapies have therapeutic potential. Through continued research, cross-disciplinary efforts involving fundamental and clinical research, and effective dissemination and discussion of results, we can address the remaining knowledge gaps to find alternative strategies to treat infections. We need to understand heterogeneity and the response to different pathogen lineages, as well as responses across different human populations. We also need insight into immune function during co-infections. We lack understanding of why some individuals clear infection and why others do not, and how pathogen, environmental or other factors influence this balance. We need to know how to push the balance in favour of the former and we need tools to predict who falls into which category, as well as how best to personalize treatment strategies.

Pathogenesis is an interactive process. The host responds to the pathogen, and in this co-evolutionary framework, the pathogen responds to the host. We cannot fully understand pathogen biology without studying it in the context of the host response, making this aspect of host–microbe interactions a key part of our journal scope. We seek to publish fundamental, preclinical and [clinical work](https://www.nature.com/nmicrobiol/editorial-policies/clinical-research) that addresses the outstanding questions in the field and draws us closer to solutions that reduce the global burden of infectious diseases.

Highlights
----------

*   •This review evaluates studies on machine learning for HPI prediction, categorizing by host, pathogen, algorithms, and metrics. 
*   •Findings reveal that tree-based algorithms like Random Forest and Gradient Boosting in HPI prediction, while deep learning needs more labeled data. 
*   •The review identifies gaps in dataset standardization and model interpretability, stressing the need for better quality and transparency in AI. 

Abstract
--------

### Background

Diseases caused by pathogenic microorganisms pose a persistent global health challenge. Pathogens exploit host mechanisms through intricate molecular interactions. Understanding these host-pathogen interactions (HPIs), particularly protein-protein interactions (PPIs), is crucial for developing therapeutic strategies. While experimental approaches are essential, they are often labor-intensive and costly. Researchers have been able to predict HPIs more efficiently due to recent advances in artificial intelligence and machine learning. However, existing reviews lack a systematic evaluation of different machine learning methodologies and their effectiveness.

### Methods

This scoping review critically examines recent studies on machine learning-based Host-Pathogen Interaction (HPI) prediction, categorizing them by host and pathogen types, machine learning algorithms, and key evaluation metrics. The methodology is based on the study beginning with a preliminary search in reputable using key phrases related to host-pathogen interactions from 2019 to 2024. This process yielded 46 relevant articles, from which 30 were selected for review after evaluating titles and abstracts.

### Results

Our findings indicate that tree-based algorithms, particularly Random Forest and Gradient Boosting, are the most prevalent in Host-Pathogen Interaction (HPI) prediction. The filter articles were categorized by host and pathogen type and further subdivided into four subcategories based on the prediction type and machine learning algorithms: classic, tree-based, vector-based, and neural network algorithms. Convolutional and recurrent neural networks are among the deep learning models that demonstrate promising accuracy, but they require a lot of labeled data for effective training. Additionally, the analysis uncovers significant gaps in dataset standardization and model interpretability, which pose challenges to the broader applicability of these predictive models.

### Conclusion

In this review, we emphasize the potential of machine learning in HPI prediction and highlight the important challenges that must be addressed to improve predictive accuracy. Unlike previous reviews, our study systematically compares different computational approaches, offering a roadmap for future research. The findings emphasize the importance of dataset quality, feature selection, and model transparency in advancing AI-driven pathogen research.

1. Introduction
---------------

The factors contributing to the disease are highly diverse regarding biochemical structure and genetic makeup. Due to minor changes in dynamic ecological balances, these factors stem from various sources and only occasionally surpass their threshold. Climatic changes and deviations from ecological equilibrium, along with other yet undiscovered dimensions, lead to the emergence of new infectious diseases ([Lahlali et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0065)). These instances render widespread outbreaks more probable. In order to combat and prevent future pandemics, researchers are striving to enhance their understanding of infectious diseases, particularly the interaction between pathogens (pathogenic microorganisms) and hosts ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)). Host-pathogen interaction (HPI) is a term used to describe how pathogens, such as viruses, bacteria, prion, fungus, and viroid, interact with their hosts. Pathogens are able to adapt to changes and find different strategies to survive and infect the host ([Poluri et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0100)). These infectious agents are responsible for causing diseases when the host's immune system is unable to mount an effective defense. Investigating host-pathogen interactions provides insights into critical questions regarding pathogen functionality, mechanisms of entry into the host through biological barriers, and their ability to persist in hosts that may be undergoing treatment or vaccination against them ([Syed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0140)). The dynamics of host and pathogen interactions can be analyzed at various levels: population level (such as viral infections within human populations), organismal level (where pathogens invade individual hosts), and molecular level (including the binding of pathogen proteins to receptors on human cells) ([Sen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0115)). This field began with scattered research on a pathogen and its interaction with the host. The initial investigations into host-pathogen interactions considered environmental factors such as light, temperature, season, and the population of pathogens- hosts ([Syed et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0140)). Later on, organisms such as _C. elegans_ and _D. melanogaster_ were identified as model organisms for studying pathogen behavior in other complex hosts (such as humans) due to their simple body plan, well-known genome structure, and short life cycle. Specific proteins and protein clusters were gradually identified to participate in host-pathogen interactions ([Tecle and Troemel, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0155); [Henry et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0045)). Furthermore, with recent advancements in imaging techniques and molecular biology, specific classifications for the mechanisms of host-pathogen interactions have emerged ([Gómez Borrego and Torrent Burgas, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0040)). Researchers in this field are attempting to use new techniques to examine and identify interactions between a pathogen and its host. Pathogens infiltrate host cells to replicate and spread, engaging in a series of protein-protein interactions (PPIs) with the host. These interactions are typically termed host-pathogen interactions ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)).

As an example of host-pathogen interactions, the mechanism of SARS-CoV-2 entry into human cells can be highlighted. The virus utilizes its spike (S) protein to bind to the ACE2 receptor on human cells, including those in the lungs, heart, and intestines. This binding occurs via the receptor-binding domain (RBD) within the S1 subunit, facilitating viral entry. Subsequently, host proteases, such as TMPRSS2, cleave the spike protein at specific sites, activating the S2 subunit, which promotes membrane fusion and enables the release of the viral RNA genome into the cytoplasm of the host cell ([Steiner et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0135)). Mutations such as N501Y enhance RBD binding affinity, increasing viral transmissibility ([Sarkar and Madabhavi, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0105)). Furthermore, viral binding to ACE2 reduces receptor levels, disrupting angiotensin II degradation and exacerbating inflammatory responses such as cytokine storms ([Bar-On et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0010)). These molecular mechanisms underscore the complexity of host-pathogen interactions and highlight the significance of investigating these interactions for developing effective therapeutic strategies.

Understanding the biological mechanisms of entry and proliferation of pathogens may contribute to developing broad strategies for combating diseases. However, it should be noted that identifying host-pathogen interactions at the mechanistic level is often a laborious, experimental, and costly endeavor. Moreover, due to the adaptability of pathogens, especially viruses, deciphering host-pathogen interaction mechanisms is akin to pursuing a moving target ([Lahlali et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0065)). One of the tools that has given researchers hope in the timely identification of host-pathogen interactions is machine learning algorithms and artificial intelligence. While empirical validation and evaluation remain imperative, computational techniques in the field of artificial intelligence may significantly reduce the number of experiments required to identify host-pathogen interactions through prediction ([Yang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0190)). The field of host-pathogen interactions predicts an important role in designing rational therapeutic interventions, such as drugs. It's possible for experimental methods to be time-consuming, cumbersome, and costly. Testing every possibility can take a significant amount of time. Machine learning can assist prediction methods in overcoming such challenges. They can initially be used to predict hypothetical data that fulfills specific conditions. Then, the predicted set can be empirically validated, requiring much less time and resources ([Kshirsagar et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0060)).

2. Research background
----------------------

### 2.1. Predicting host-pathogen interactions

The interactions between hosts and pathogens are determinant factors in the dynamics of infectious diseases. These interactions are mainly driven by protein-protein interactions (PPIs), which govern each phase of disease progression, starting from the initial invasion of the pathogen to its eventual establishment within the host ([Ul Qamar et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0165)). Proteins are large macromolecules made up of chains of amino acids. Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are essential for nearly all cellular biological and biochemical processes. The majority of proteins carry out their functions through interactions with other proteins. These functions encompass nutrient transport, enzymatic reactions, immune responses, cell-to-cell communication, and tissue regeneration. Therefore, precise PPI identification is essential for understanding cellular physiology ([Soleymani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0130)). In other words, predicting host-pathogen interactions involves predicting protein-protein interactions between the host and the pathogen based on recognizing protein sequences and infectious phenotypes (signs and symptoms) ([Liu-Wei et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0075)).

Moreover, monitoring and analyzing protein-protein interactions (PPIs) can yield valuable candidates for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in medicine, thereby aiding in the development of new drugs ([Trepte et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0160)). Therefore, the challenge of predicting PPIs is a fundamental research topic in systems biology and has thus garnered increased attention in recent years.

Various computational models have been proposed to overcome these drawbacks so that protein-protein interaction pairs can be systematically identified ([Hu et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0050)). In other words, the most extensive method by which a pathogen interacts with a host is Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI). In order to attack the host, pathogen proteins interact with host proteins. The host and its environment can be affected by pathogen proteins in different ways. They can directly interact with host protein(s), affecting subsequent reaction cascades and impairing the host's natural function(s). They can even jeopardize the host's immune defense by misleading and weakening it. They may exploit elements of a collapsing anaerobic environment in the damaged host from immune system attacks. Therefore, predicting potential PPIs between pathogens and their host(s) is of utmost importance ([Durmuş Tekir et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0035)). Various computational exist to predict whether a host protein can interact with a pathogenic protein or vice versa. Current methods for predicting PPIs are generally classified into two categories: laboratory-based and computational ([Murmu et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0090)). Laboratory based methods have their own set of limitations. For instance, in experiments the first issue related to PPIs is usually the time-consuming and effort-intensive nature of these controls, which do not always lead to effective detection of the protein-protein interactions (PPI). Second, the PPI data obtained from these methods are in general incomplete imposed by the fact that assays have their limitations. Lastly, sufficient evidence for high rates of false positives and the like are among predictive results ([Yin et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0195)).

One of these approaches is the prediction based on physicochemical information. This information includes molecular weight, electric charge, solubility, isoelectric point, etc. The physicochemical information-based approach for predicting host-pathogen protein interactions is widely used due to its simplicity. However, this method has weaknesses because the predicted interactions based on this approach may not be significantly reliable due to neglecting other biological features. Therefore, there is a need for more information to increase prediction accuracy ([Tahir et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0145)). Another predictive method is structure-based prediction. When two proteins exhibit structural similarities to a known interacting protein pair, it can be inferred that these proteins may interact in a comparable manner. Another approach is motif-based prediction, which predicts the probability of interaction between two proteins based on the presence of specific motifs in their structure or sequence ([Palhamkhani et al., 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0095)). Motifs are small parts of protein structures or sequences that have specific functions; for example, specific motifs may be responsible for binding to other proteins. By identifying and recognizing similar motifs in proteins, it is possible to predict whether these proteins may interact with each other or not ([Sen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0115)).

### 2.2. Machine learning algorithms

The field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) emerged from efforts to create frameworks for machines with “intelligent features.” Machine Learning (ML), a subset of AI, has greatly contributed to this development by providing practical tools. Typically, a machine learning model is trained on a dataset containing features and variables that influence the target problem, using either supervised or unsupervised methods. Supervised learning involves independent variables (X) and dependent variables (Y), where Y represents a known target feature. These supervised learning techniques are particularly effective for automating the analysis of biological data, where defining precise rules for relevant events can be challenging. ([Yakimovich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0180)).

In recent years, the development of machine learning algorithms has led to the emergence of new methodologies. Alongside classical machine learning algorithms, deep learning algorithms can be considered as a sophisticated evolution of machine learning algorithms. Deep learning has become highly prominent today, and the reason for this can be attributed to achieving results that seemed impossible before ([Chen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0025)).

In recent years, machine learning and deep learning have gained significant attention as subsets of artificial intelligence. Machine learning enables algorithms to be autonomously adapted and enhanced based on incoming data, generating desired outputs without the need for human intervention. Deep learning, which is a subset of machine learning, employs multiple algorithms that each provide a different interpretation of the input data. This hierarchical structure is referred to as artificial neural networks, which endeavor to emulate the functionality of neural networks found in the human brain ([Mahapatra and Sahu, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0080)). Machine learning techniques were only occasionally utilized in the analysis of interactions between pathogens and their hosts in the field of biology before artificial intelligence was utilized. The use of machine learning and artificial intelligence techniques for analyzing host-pathogen interactions has become increasingly important due to the emergence of new diseases. One specific application of machine learning algorithms is the investigation of pathogens and protein interaction sequences to predict host-pathogen interactions, which has garnered significant interest among researchers ([Kaundal et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0055)).

The field of PPI prediction has been well-developed using machine learning models due to the availability of a vast amount of protein data generated by advanced technologies in recent years. Some researchers, such as Tang et al. provide a comprehensive review of machine learning-based prediction methods. The machine learning models used in these methods and the details of protein data representation in their research are specified. The discussion of the development process of machine learning-based methods has been raised to understand the potential advancements in predicting PPIs ([Tang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1567134825000401#bb0150)).

3. Research procedure
---------------------

In this research, considering the importance of utilizing new approaches in predicting host-pathogen interactions, efforts have been made to examine some recent studies in the field of host-pathogen interaction prediction using machine learning algorithms. To this end, the reviewed research has been analyzed and investigated in terms of the data sets used, the type of host and pathogen, the type of algorithm employed for prediction, independent variables for prediction, model evaluation indices, and how research results are presented. This research aims to categorize recent studies in the field of host-pathogen interaction to serve as a suitable guide for researchers. For this purpose, A systematic and step-by-step process was employed to collect articles related to the topic of “Prediction of Host”. ## About this Research Topic

Submission closed

### Background

Microorganisms interact with larger organisms in diverse modes, which can span from beneficial symbiosis to harmful pathogenicity. The complex network of interactions within a host significantly influences the progression and outcome of diseases. Understanding these interactions from a one health perspective is therefore essential for developing effective treatments and preventive measures against infectious diseases. This area of study also seeks to bridge the existing knowledge gaps by integrating recent findings on internal host mechanisms and pathogen traits that drive disease processes.  
  
The goal of this Research Topic is to provide comprehensive insights into the interactions between a range of pathogenic microorganisms—including bacteria, yeasts, protozoa, and parasites—and their host organisms. It emphasizes the One Health approach, recognizing the interconnected health of humans, animals, and our shared environment. By focusing on holistic data integration and methodological advancements, this Research Topic aims to uncover new strategies for global disease prevention and control that are less susceptible to resistance.  
  
To further our understanding of these complex interactions, the scope of this Research Topic is defined by its emphasis on the One Health perspective. It aims to explore how environmental changes, animal health, and human activities collectively influence disease dynamics.  
  
We welcome original research and review articles that address, but are not limited to, the following themes:  
- Advanced diagnostic tools for identifying and managing zoonotic and emerging infectious diseases.  
- Effects of environmental changes on host-pathogen interactions and their impact on disease progression.  
- Mechanisms of disease, cross-species transmission, and integrated health management for improving overall health outcomes.  
- Collaborative research and interdisciplinary strategies that integrate human, animal, and environmental health.  
- Surveillance systems that monitor disease trends across human, animal, and environment for outbreak preparedness, early detection and coordinated response.

**Keywords:** host-pathogen, one health, human health, animal health, surveillance

Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial Commensalism, Colonization, Infection, and Disease - PMC  Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial Commensalism, Colonization, Infection, and Disease Here, we critically review the origin and historical evolution of key concepts used to describe the outcome of host-microbe interactions, namely, infection, commensalism, colonization, persistence, infection, and disease. Although the concept of the carrier state may have undermined the pathogen-centered view of microbial pathogenesis, it invoked a mutability that paved the way for defining host-microbe interaction as a regulated relationship. Thus, rather than providing definitions based on the outcome of host-microbe interaction, the current lexicon puts forth terms that are primarily intended to define a pathogen and whether or not it causes infection and/or disease.

Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial Commensalism, Colonization, Infection, and Disease
==========================================================================================================

Most of the terminology used to define the host-microbe interaction has been in use for nearly a century. Early in this period, microbes were thought to be primary aggressors that governed the host-pathogen interaction, resulting in disease. Later, new information about the attributes of microbes and their hosts resulted in the understanding that the host-pathogen interaction does not always result in disease. This recognition, in turn, led to the introduction of terms to explain states in which microbes exist within hosts without causing overt disease and why some microbes only cause disease in certain hosts. Commensal, carrier state, and opportunist were terms put forth to account for microbes and conditions that were sometimes associated with disease but for which Koch's postulates could not be fulfilled for one reason or another. Most of these terms were originally proposed to describe the behavior of particular microbes, rather than to define a more general host-microbe relationship.

Recently, we reviewed the concepts of virulence and pathogenicity and described how the definitions for these terms changed over the years as microbiologists tried to find ways to convey that microbial pathogenesis reflects an interaction between two entities, host and pathogen ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B7)). Based on the concept that host damage was the most relevant outcome of the host-pathogen interaction, we proposed revisions to the definitions of the terms pathogen, pathogenicity, and virulence ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B7)). However, the proposed framework suggested a need to reexamine the terms used to define the outcomes of host-microbe interactions. Here, we critically review the origin and historical evolution of key concepts used to describe the outcome of host-microbe interactions, namely, infection, commensalism, colonization, persistence, infection, and disease. We propose that the meaning of these terms can be clarified by placing them in the context of the damage framework put forth previously ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B7)).

LEXICON OF MICROBIAL PATHOGENESIS
---------------------------------

Once the germ theory of disease was accepted, microbes were considered to be pathogens if they met the stipulations of Koch's postulate. However, it rapidly became apparent that (i) although there are many microbes, most human infections were caused by only a few; (ii) some microbes were classified as pathogens although they did not cause disease in every host; and (iii) some microbes were classified as nonpathogens, although they did cause disease in certain hosts (for an early review, see reference [56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B56)). In addition, it became evident that normal individuals harbored, in their mouth, gut, and skin, large numbers of microbes that did not cause disease. New ideas and terminology, heretofore referred to collectively as a lexicon, were devised to accommodate this information.

By the early twentieth century, it was apparent that pathogenicity was neither an invariant nor a stable characteristic of most microbes and that the acquisition of pathogenic microbes was not necessarily synonymous with disease. In the laboratory, the successful attenuation of pathogens revealed that virulence could be increased or decreased by animal passage and/or in vitro culture (for a review of early experimentation, see reference [4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B4)). This scientific advance eventually led to the development of vaccines that controlled many of the major childhood diseases of the past. In the clinical realm, it was recognized that a microbe responsible for an epidemic disease could be isolated from both symptomatic and asymptomatic individuals in the midst of an epidemic. For example, Henrici noted that only a small number of individuals developed disease when epidemics of cerebrospinal meningitis (_Neisseria meningiditis_) occurred in a community, whereas others carried the bacteria but remained healthy (i.e., carriers); however, the majority were neither sick nor carriers ([18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B18)). Based on the ability to culture staphylococci and streptococci from most people despite the absence of any manifestation of disease, Kolmer proposed a condition called subinfection ([26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B26)). This concept blurred the distinction between pathogens and nonpathogens and challenged a prevailing concept of microbial pathogenesis that was based on Koch's postulate. The recognition of the carrier state was problematic vis a vis the terminology of the time, because a necessary condition for associating certain pathogens with particular diseases was that the converse of Koch's postulate should be fulfilled; the causative microbe should not be found in unaffected individuals ([55](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B55)). However, the carrier state had not been accounted for in the formulation of Koch's postulate, and the recovery of pathogens from healthy hosts challenged the second part of the postulate that a parasite “occurs in no other disease as a fortuitous and nonpathogenic parasite” (postulate wording as suggested by Evans [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B12)]).

The description of the carrier state confused existing definitions of pathogens. To both explain the carrier state and preserve the distinction between pathogenic and nonpathogenic microbes, the concept that host and pathogen could adapt to one another was put forth. Karsner and Ecker described this adaptation as involving changes to both host and microbe, such that the host suffered no damage and the microbe was resistant to the immune system ([25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B25)). Similarly, Park and Williams described the carrier state as a commensal development by the pathogenic microorganism ([36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B36)). Later, the view emerged that the carrier state was transient and true pathogens elicited immune responses that eliminated the pathogens, whereas commensals did not ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)). Asymptomatic carriage of pathogens that elicited immunity was considered to be a benefit to the host that was tolerated at the risk of serious disease ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)). Conversely, the recognition that a carrier state could follow resolution of clinical disease, e.g., for _Salmonella enterica_ serovar Typhi, illustrated that in certain hosts there was a persistent risk of transmission, and possibly reacquisition, of infection. Another variation of the carrier state with great clinical importance is “microbial persistence,” which refers to the situation in which susceptible pathogens are not eradicated despite antimicrobial therapy ([31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B31)). The carrier state remains poorly understood, and, as noted by Smith, this area of study is relatively underrepresented in microbial pathogenesis research ([41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B41)). Although the concept of the carrier state may have undermined the pathogen-centered view of microbial pathogenesis, it invoked a mutability that paved the way for defining host-microbe interaction as a regulated relationship.

Intrinsic to the terms commensalism, colonization, and the carrier state was the concept that some microbes had the capacity to persist in their hosts. According to a model proposed by Blaser ([3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B3)), ongoing interactions between host and microbe have different outcomes that depend upon regulation of the host-microbe relationship and coevolution of host and microbe favors an outcome in which the cost of eliminating the microbe is high. In the late twentieth century, studies of bacterial pathogenesis led to the identification of molecular differences between pathogenic and nonpathogenic microbes, and this engendered the belief that pathogens and nonpathogens were intrinsically different ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B14), [15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B15), [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B17), [41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B41)). In this regard, historical definitions of terms used in the field of microbial pathogenesis have focused on distinctions between pathogens and nonpathogens rather than on the frequently divergent outcomes that often characterize different host-microbe relationships. The existence of the latter underscores the need for terminology that describes host-microbe interaction, rather than pathogen-specific characteristics.

Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1) lists historical definitions for terms commonly used in the field of microbial pathogenesis. These definitions reveal a conceptual evolution that paralleled emerging concepts in microbial pathogenesis and clinical infectious diseases, albeit in some conflict with current concepts of infection and immunity. For example, the implication that states of colonization or commensalism do not invoke an immune response are inconsistent with evidence that normal microflora can elicit specific antibody responses (reviewed in reference [46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B46)). In fact, antibodies to _Staphylococcus aureus_ and _Candida albicans_ are frequent in healthy individuals that harbor these microbes without disease ([16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B16), [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B38), [39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B39)), and carriers of group A _Streptococcus_ often have titers of antibody to streptococcal antigens which are attributed to prior infection ([47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B47)). Moreover, the presence of antibodies can represent outcomes as diverse as ongoing viral replication (e.g., in human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] natural or vaccine-elicited immunity, latency, carriage, and cross-reactivity with antigens of another microbe or an unknown antigen. However, if the presence of antibodies implies present or past infection, the view that certain states (e.g., colonization and the carrier state) are precursors to infection requires modification. Thus, rather than providing definitions based on the outcome of host-microbe interaction, the current lexicon puts forth terms that are primarily intended to define a pathogen and whether or not it causes infection and/or disease.

In summary, practically all past treatises on the subject of host-microbe interaction made some distinction between colonization, infection, and disease. However, the basis for the definitions of these terms was embedded in examples of specific microbes rather than in a general framework. The lack of such a framework has led to some ambiguity in the meaning of these terms.

IMPACT OF CHANGING SPECTRUM OF INFECTIOUS DISEASES ON LEXICON
-------------------------------------------------------------

Though many historical definitions (Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1)) are adequate when considered in the context of specific microbes and diseases, most definitions do not account for the varied outcomes of the host-microbe interaction. Early definitions were formulated following the rapid acquisition of new knowledge in the late nineteenth century. At that time, the distinction between pathogenic, nonpathogenic, and commensal organisms may have been more clear-cut, since the majority of human hosts probably had what would be considered normal immunity today, as those with immune impairment were unlikely to survive childhood. The adjective classical has been applied to pathogens that were major causes of infectious disease in the past ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)). However, the introduction of sanitation, serum therapy, vaccination, and then effective antimicrobial therapy reduced the prevalence of and mortality from classical pathogens, though such microbes remain a major health problem in underdeveloped regions. By the 1950s, neoplasia and inflammatory and degenerative diseases, rather than infectious diseases, were thought to be the major medical problems in industrialized nations. However, the development of corticosteroid and cytotoxic therapies, organ transplantation, invasive surgeries, and ultimately the catastrophe of the HIV epidemic, produced a new population of human hosts with impaired immune systems ([27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B27), [51](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B51)) that were vulnerable to infections with various microbes previously thought to be nonpathogenic ([2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B2)).

A major change in the prevalence of certain pathogens occurred in the twentieth century. This was exemplified by the shift in the etiologic agents of bloodstream infections from gram-positive to gram-negative microbes in the early part of the century, and then back again to gram-positive and fungal microbes toward the end of the century ([11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B11), [14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B14)). The primary cause of these shifts was antibiotic selection. Since multiple microbes may be present in the hospitalized setting, infection represents an outcome of selective pressures in the context of the host-microbe relationship. Unexpected outcomes of the host-microbe relationship are best illustrated by the increased prevalence of unusual infections in individuals with advanced HIV infection. For example, by the middle of the first decade of the HIV epidemic in the early 1990s, _Cryptococcus neoformans_ was the most frequent cause of meningitis in New York City, when the more than 1,000 cases of cryptococcal meningitis ([9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B9)) outnumbered the 285 cases of meningitis caused by all bacterial pathogens ([29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B29)). This reflects the influence of the HIV epidemic upon the spectrum of infectious diseases in a population as well as the impact of the introduction of an effective vaccine against an important pathogen, namely, _Haemophilus influenzae_ type b.

The recognition that microbes thought to be nonpathogens caused disease in certain hosts challenged the definitions of saprophyte and commensal. As a result, additional terms were added to the lexicon in an attempt to find terminology that could accommodate the new medical and scientific findings (Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1)). In addition to the terms listed in Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1), other terms and adjectives used to describe microbes and their interactions with the host in the literature include pure saprophytes ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57)), pure parasite ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57)), half parasite ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57)), classical ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)), persister ([31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B31)), nosocomial ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B1)), iatrogenic ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B1)), convalescent carrier ([48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B48)), precocious carrier ([48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B48)), chronic carrier ([48](ht

enriched_points.expanded_content: Continued research into host-pathogen interactions is vital for understanding disease mechanisms, developing effective treatments, and mitigating the impacts of emerging infectious diseases.

Advancements in technologies like machine learning and AI are revolutionizing the prediction of host-pathogen interactions, enhancing our ability to identify therapeutic targets and improve treatment strategies.

Interdisciplinary collaboration across sectors is essential for enhancing public health responses, improving disease surveillance, and developing integrated strategies to combat infectious threats globally.
